Article.ArticleTitle: Comprehensive chemical molecular characterization of petroleum-contaminated soils: Unraveling physical migration and biotransformation processes in the vadose and saturated zones.

Abstract.AbstractText: Conventional investigations of petroleum contamination have typically focused on a limited number of regulated compounds, failing to employ comprehensive chemical molecular characterization techniques to have a more extensive elucidation of petroleum fate and transport in soil and groundwater environment. This study employed quantitative molecular characterization techniques to explore the spatial distribution as well as fate and transport of petroleum over a large (∼50,000 m2) light non-aqueous phase liquid plume. A total of 388 soil samples were collected from different depths of 29 soil cores covering the upper vadose zone, smear zone and saturated zone of the site. We found that the distribution patterns of total petroleum hydrocarbon (TPH) were governed by the spilled oil composition, with gasoline-range TPHC6-C9 concentrating in the smear zone, while diesel-range TPHC10-C40 exhibited wider vertical dispersal. Compared to petroleum hydrocarbons, methyl tert-butyl ether (MTBE) and tert-amyl methyl ether (TAME) penetrated deeply into the saturated zone and emerged as predominant risk drivers owing to high solubility and low biodegradability. We also found substantial loss of soluble monoaromatics in saturated zone, and volatile ≤ C8 hydrocarbons lost in vadose zone, while C3-C4 alkylbenzenes and ≥C9 hydrocarbons exhibited greater persistence in soil. Fine-textured soils (silt and clay) effectively preserved petroleum molecular fingerprints, whereas in sand, extensive physical and biological transformation altered the original molecular signatures. This study demonstrates the value of comprehensive chemical molecular characterization in understanding petroleum fate beyond conventional monitoring approaches, providing insights for risk assessment and targeted remediation.
